name: simple_model
positioning: A model-portable deterministic control plane that understands living software systems, compiles structural intent into proof-carrying macros, and safely evolves one repository or an entire repository fleet.
north_star: Given a large half-built project, simple_model should reconstruct a trustworthy executable model of its code and runtime behavior, identify high-value structural improvements, synthesize or select reusable macros, prove their safety, execute them transactionally, and continuously verify that the project becomes easier to evolve.

current_strengths: The project already has struct.json, semantic graphs, dynamic-edge evidence, macro contracts, plan search, rollback metadata, benchmark gates, plugin commands, and MCP exposure.
The deterministic control plane is model-portable and already constrains AI to bounded leaf work.
The repository can ingest half-built projects, issue interface-stability reports, and produce replayable proof bundles.
current_gaps: The current release reports 13 macro operators but zero apply-capable and zero simulate-capable operators; most macro intelligence still stops at advice.
The current self-analysis classifies all 94 discovered interfaces as blocked, so the evidence model is too conservative or too weak to support useful automation.
The macro gauntlet has only four cases, which is insufficient evidence for cross-project production claims.
The project has many commands and reports, but no unified structural query language, lossless edit representation, always-on intelligence daemon, or multi-repository campaign executor.
Runtime evidence exists as a concept but is not yet a continuous feedback loop that can discharge static uncertainty and promote macros safely.

research_basis.name: OpenRewrite recipes and Lossless Semantic Trees
research_basis.lesson: Production refactoring needs typed, composable transformations over format-preserving semantic trees.

research_basis.name: CodeQL data-flow analysis
research_basis.lesson: AST structure alone is insufficient; local/global data flow, taint state, barriers, and path evidence must become first-class graph facts.

research_basis.name: Tree-sitter query syntax
research_basis.lesson: A small structural query DSL can make polyglot pattern matching reusable and explainable.

research_basis.name: Clang AST matchers and refactoring engine
research_basis.lesson: Matchers, bound nodes, typed replacements, and rule-specific initiation requirements are proven building blocks for deterministic source transformation.

research_basis.name: OpenTelemetry semantic conventions
research_basis.lesson: Runtime traces become portable evidence only when normalized into stable semantic conventions.

research_basis.name: Nix reproducible builds
research_basis.lesson: Macro proof requires hermetic execution and reproducible artifacts, not merely a successful local test run.

research_basis.name: SWE-EVO long-horizon evolution benchmark
research_basis.lesson: Long-running multi-file evolution remains substantially harder than isolated issue repair and needs explicit state, checkpoints, and regression preservation.

research_basis.name: Long-running agent harness design
research_basis.lesson: The harness and evaluator loop are part of capability; persistent progress, independent evaluation, and resumable context must be designed explicitly.

operating_invariants: Deterministic scripts own structural decisions, write sets, scheduling, promotion, rollback, and release gates.
AI may explain, propose parameters, classify unresolved domain intent, or generate review-only candidates; it may not directly promote or apply a macro.
No macro becomes apply-capable without typed matching, bounded effects, idempotency, rollback, affected-test coverage, counterexamples, and benchmark evidence.
Unknown dynamic behavior lowers trust and triggers evidence gathering; it is never silently interpreted as safe.
Every decisive artifact is content-addressed, replayable offline, and records parser/runtime/tool provenance.
Daily development uses affected checks and caches; release certification uses hermetic full checks and adversarial benchmarks.
Every plugin command has a stable JSON contract, terminal rendering, tests, MCP exposure where appropriate, and a release-gate owner.
definition_of_done: Named generators, schemas, fixtures, reports, plugin commands, and tests exist and are connected rather than being documentation-only placeholders.
Acceptance metrics are measured on labeled fixtures and at least one repository not used while implementing the feature.
Failure, timeout, partial evidence, dirty worktree, rollback, and resume paths are covered.
The command manifest, skill reference, MCP surface, terminal report, package manifest, and release SLO are updated when the capability is user-facing.
A roadmap item remains pending if its evidence is indirect, only self-referential, or cannot be replayed from a clean checkout.

waves.name: Executable Truth Foundation
waves.exit_gate: A single typed project graph and query language can explain symbols, types, control/data flow, runtime evidence, ownership, tests, and interfaces with stable identities.

waves.name: Production Rewrite Kernel
waves.exit_gate: At least six representative macros can perform lossless native rewrites in isolated workspaces and prove formatting, idempotency, test selection, rollback, and replay.

waves.name: Macro Wisdom Compiler
waves.exit_gate: Repeated examples, findings, and migration specifications can be compiled into review-only macros and refined by deterministic counterexamples before promotion.

waves.name: High-Leverage Macro Packs
waves.exit_gate: Common repository-evolution workflows are available as convenient, parameterized, production-tested macro packs rather than bespoke scripts.

waves.name: Living Runtime Intelligence
waves.exit_gate: Runtime observations continuously discharge static uncertainty, update trust states, and verify behavioral equivalence without allowing probes to mutate production state.

waves.name: Fleet and Product Experience
waves.exit_gate: Users can take over, watch, query, optimize, and coordinate changes across large multi-repository systems through one CLI/plugin workflow.

waves.name: Evidence, Scale, and Ecosystem
waves.exit_gate: Claims are supported by a large external corpus, long-horizon evolution benchmarks, hermetic replay, signed macro packs, and a strict v2 release gate.

todos.goal: Replace self-reported capability counts with an executable maturity audit that proves which commands and macros analyze, simulate, apply, rollback, replay, and generalize on external fixtures.
acceptance: Every command and macro receives a maturity level backed by direct artifact and test evidence.
The audit distinguishes report-only, review-only, simulate-capable, apply-capable, externally-proven, and fleet-proven behavior.
A claimed capability fails closed when its fixture, rollback, or external evidence is missing.
Release reports show deltas from the previous maturity baseline.

todos.goal: Unify syntax, symbols, types, imports, calls, control flow, data flow, interfaces, runtime edges, tests, ownership, build targets, and deployment resources into one versioned graph.
acceptance: All node and edge identities remain stable across unrelated edits and path-preserving renames.
Graph partitions can be updated independently and merged deterministically.
Each edge carries evidence class, confidence, provenance, freshness, and invalidation keys.
The graph supports polyglot call paths and contract paths across repository boundaries.

todos.goal: Replace ad hoc confidence numbers with an evidence lattice that can combine, contradict, expire, and discharge parser, build, test, runtime, ownership, and human-contract evidence.
acceptance: Evidence joins are associative, commutative, deterministic, and provenance-preserving.
Contradictory static and runtime evidence produces an explicit conflict state rather than an averaged score.
Freshness and invalidation propagate only to dependent graph partitions.
Every blocked interface or macro emits the minimal evidence needed to change its decision.

todos.goal: Create a compact declarative Macro Query Language for matching graph motifs, AST nodes, paths, data-flow states, runtime evidence, and project policies without writing one-off jq pipelines.
acceptance: MQL supports typed node patterns, graph traversal, captures, negative patterns, quantifiers, evidence predicates, and bounded path queries.
Queries compile to a normalized deterministic plan with cost estimates and an explain trace.
The engine rejects unbounded or ambiguous queries before execution.
At least 30 existing repository checks are re-expressed in MQL with identical results.

todos.goal: Define a language-neutral lossless edit IR that preserves formatting and comments while carrying typed captures, symbol identities, preconditions, effects, inverse operations, and source maps.
acceptance: No-op rendering is byte-identical for supported languages.
Each edit references stable captures rather than raw line numbers alone.
Inverse edits restore byte-identical content and graph hashes.
Overlapping or stale edits are rejected with exact conflict locations.

todos.goal: Provide a stable adapter SDK so language parsers, LSPs, tree-sitter queries, native refactoring tools, and external recipe engines can contribute normalized facts and edits.
acceptance: Adapters negotiate capabilities, versions, languages, provenance, timeouts, and sandbox requirements.
Malformed, partial, nondeterministic, or stale adapter output is rejected.
Conformance fixtures cover parser-only, query-only, rewrite, and runtime-evidence adapters.
Optional backends never weaken the fail-closed baseline when unavailable.

todos.goal: Execute MQL structural patterns against native and tree-sitter ASTs with typed captures, symbol resolution, local style context, and match stability checks.
acceptance: Matches use syntax and symbol identity rather than regex as the primary mechanism.
Equivalent formatting and harmless syntax variation preserve match results.
Ambiguous captures are reported and cannot reach apply mode.
Matcher precision and recall are measured on labeled polyglot fixtures.

todos.goal: Implement production native rewrite backends for TypeScript, Python, Go, and Rust using the lossless edit IR and language-appropriate tooling.
acceptance: Each backend supports symbol rename, import editing, declaration insertion/removal, call-site rewrite, and signature migration.
Backends preserve comments, local formatting, encoding, and newline conventions.
Every backend has parse-after-write, typecheck/build, idempotency, inverse, and malformed-input fixtures.
Fallback text edits remain review-only and are never silently labeled native.

todos.goal: Add production rewrite support for Java/Kotlin and C/C++ through OpenRewrite-compatible and Clang-compatible adapters.
acceptance: JVM rewrites use type-attributed semantic trees when the backend is installed.
C/C++ rewrites require compilation database evidence and reject unresolved translation units.
Adapter absence produces an actionable review-only decision rather than a false success.
Fixtures include templates, macros, generated code, mixed Java/Kotlin modules, and build failures.

todos.goal: Plan minimal patches that respect repository-local style, generated regions, ownership boundaries, formatter scopes, and merge-conflict probability.
acceptance: The planner minimizes touched lines and files under semantic correctness constraints.
Generated, vendored, ignored, or explicitly protected regions are excluded by default.
Formatter execution is scoped to changed syntax regions where tooling supports it.
Patch plans report conflict risk and alternative lower-conflict schedules.

todos.goal: Run macros in hermetic isolated workspaces with declared tools, environment, resource limits, network policy, content-addressed inputs, checkpoints, and reproducible outputs.
acceptance: The runner supports local sandbox, container, and optional Nix execution with equivalent contracts.
Undeclared network, environment, filesystem, or tool access fails the transaction.
Interrupted runs resume from verified checkpoints without replaying completed writes.
Two clean runs over identical inputs produce identical decision and artifact hashes.

todos.goal: Give macro authors one command that generates fixtures and certifies match precision, bounded effects, idempotency, rollback, composition, test impact, performance, and external generality.
acceptance: Certificates are content-addressed, signed locally, and invalidated by macro or fixture changes.
Negative, adversarial, partial-parse, dirty-worktree, and rollback fixtures are mandatory.
Certification emits exact remediation for every failed proof obligation.
Uncertified macros cannot enter trusted packs or apply mode.

todos.goal: Compute semantic read/write effects, symbol aliases, generated artifacts, build outputs, and commutativity so macro plans can safely parallelize or reorder transformations.
acceptance: Effects include direct files, symbols, generated outputs, contracts, tests, build targets, and runtime surfaces.
Unknown aliasing blocks parallel apply but still permits isolated simulation.
The solver produces counterexamples for rejected compositions.
Parallel and serial execution are proven equivalent for accepted commuting macros.

todos.goal: Provide deterministic equivalence oracles for APIs, schemas, serialization, CLI output, database migrations, events, traces, performance budgets, and selected pure functions.
acceptance: Oracles distinguish exact, normalized, observational, bounded, and intentionally-breaking equivalence.
Nondeterministic fields can only be ignored through explicit typed normalizers.
Before/after evidence includes minimized failing inputs and graph paths.
A passing unit test suite cannot override a failing declared equivalence contract.

todos.goal: Synthesize parameterized review-only macro templates from curated before/after examples by structural differencing, capture inference, anti-unification, and negative-example checks.
acceptance: Generated templates infer parameters, required context, edit operations, postconditions, and forbidden matches.
One example can never produce an apply-capable macro.
Multiple examples with incompatible edits produce separate candidates or an explicit conflict.
Held-out examples measure synthesis precision and generalization.

todos.goal: Compile repeated MQL, CodeQL, Semgrep, compiler, linter, test, and runtime findings into deduplicated macro candidates with source-path evidence and remediation semantics.
acceptance: Findings from different tools normalize to stable semantic identities and retain original provenance.
Candidates include a deterministic matcher, edit strategy, scope, evidence requirements, and counterexamples.
Duplicate findings across tools increase evidence without duplicating work.
Security-sensitive or behavior-changing findings default to simulation and human approval.

todos.goal: Compile OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, protobuf, GraphQL, SQL schema, dependency lockfile, and configuration contract diffs into ordered migration macro plans.
acceptance: Plans separate compatible additions, deprecations, shims, breaking changes, data migrations, and consumer updates.
Cross-language producers and consumers are linked through the unified graph.
Irreversible data changes require backup, validation, and explicit approval contracts.
Plans emit staged rollout and rollback order rather than one undifferentiated patch.

todos.goal: Use failing fixtures, false matches, missed matches, type errors, runtime divergence, and rollback failures to deterministically refine macro predicates and promotion state.
acceptance: Counterexamples are minimized, hash-addressed, assigned to a proof obligation, and retained permanently.
Refinement may narrow matching or add evidence requirements but cannot auto-weaken safety policy.
Previously fixed counterexamples become mandatory regression fixtures.
Repeated unresolved counterexamples automatically demote or retire a macro.

todos.goal: Create certified meta-macros that scaffold, specialize, compose, test, document, benchmark, and package new macro families from normalized evidence.
acceptance: Meta-macros generate complete reviewable source packages, never opaque executable blobs.
Generated macros inherit certification, counterexample, documentation, and release-gate templates.
The meta-macro cannot modify its own trust policy or promotion thresholds.
Generated output is deterministic for identical evidence and parameters.

todos.goal: Manage macro versions, dependencies, compatibility, certificates, counterexamples, provenance, deprecation, revocation, promotion, and fleet eligibility in one deterministic registry.
acceptance: Resolution is deterministic and rejects incompatible or cyclic macro dependencies.
Revoked certificates immediately remove apply eligibility without deleting audit history.
Promotion requires minimum external-repository diversity in addition to fixture counts.
Registry snapshots are signed and included in proof bundles.

todos.goal: Deliver convenient production macros for symbol rename, move, visibility change, split, merge, deprecate, re-export, alias, and removal across supported languages.
acceptance: Macros update definitions, references, imports, exports, docs, tests, and generated contracts through stable symbol identities.
Dynamic references and reflection are either updated through trusted resolvers or block apply.
Deprecation macros generate compatibility aliases and removal deadlines.
At least four macros are certified apply-capable on external fixtures.

todos.goal: Automate component extraction, module split/merge, layer enforcement, dependency inversion, facade creation, cycle breaking, and ownership-boundary repair.
acceptance: Plans preserve public contracts or explicitly introduce versioned compatibility shims.
File moves update build targets, package metadata, imports, tests, ownership, and struct fragments.
Cycle-breaking choices are ranked by semantic blast radius and migration cost.
At least three macros are certified apply-capable on multi-module external fixtures.

todos.goal: Provide macros for endpoint, RPC, event, schema, CLI, and library API evolution with compatibility windows, adapters, shims, consumer migration, and staged removal.
acceptance: Macros cover additive, rename, type-change, split/merge, pagination, error-model, and authentication migrations.
Every breaking migration emits a compatibility matrix and consumer completion ledger.
Generated clients and server stubs remain synchronized with source contracts.
At least four macros are certified simulate-capable and two apply-capable.

todos.goal: Automate dependency upgrades, package-manager migrations, deprecated API replacement, lockfile regeneration, build-tool changes, and framework-version migrations.
acceptance: Plans separate mechanical edits, semantic API changes, configuration changes, and manual release-note decisions.
Lockfiles are generated only in locked hermetic environments.
Transitive vulnerabilities and license/policy constraints participate in version selection.
At least six ecosystem migrations have replayable end-to-end fixtures.

todos.goal: Normalize configuration, environment variables, secrets references, feature flags, defaults, validation, documentation, and deployment manifests across code and infrastructure.
acceptance: Macros reconcile code reads, schema declarations, defaults, docs, CI, containers, and deployment manifests.
Secret values are never recorded; only typed references and providers are modeled.
Flag removal proves branch reachability and rollout completion before deleting code.
At least three macros are certified apply-capable.

todos.goal: Generate and normalize tracing, metrics, logs, correlation IDs, error attributes, and service/resource metadata using OpenTelemetry semantic conventions.
acceptance: Instrumentation preserves behavior and does not duplicate spans, metrics, or handlers.
Semantic attributes are validated against pinned convention versions.
Sensitive-data policy blocks unsafe attribute capture.
Trace-based runtime evidence can map observations back to stable graph nodes.

todos.goal: Provide macros for error-model normalization, timeout propagation, cancellation, retry policy, resource cleanup, async migration, bounded concurrency, and idempotency keys.
acceptance: Macros model call-chain propagation and do not insert retries around non-idempotent operations without policy evidence.
Concurrency rewrites include race, cancellation, leak, and saturation fixtures.
Behavioral oracles verify error shape, timeout behavior, and observable side effects.
At least three macros are certified simulate-capable and two apply-capable.

todos.goal: Deterministically scaffold contract tests, characterization tests, property-test harnesses, fuzz targets, fixture builders, and migration assertions from interface and data-flow evidence.
acceptance: Generated tests target uncovered contracts and graph paths rather than maximizing raw test count.
Assertions are derived from explicit contracts or observed characterization baselines and are labeled accordingly.
Fuzz targets declare resource limits, seed corpora, and reproducibility metadata.
AI may fill domain-specific examples only through typed leaf tasks reviewed before merge.

todos.goal: Safely remove unreachable code, stale flags, unused exports, abandoned adapters, orphan tests, duplicate utilities, and architecture violations using static plus runtime reachability evidence.
acceptance: Deletion requires static non-reachability, dynamic observation policy, ownership policy, and affected-test proof.
Public, reflective, serialized, plugin, and externally referenced symbols default to blocked.
Duplicate consolidation proves behavior and dependency direction before replacement.
At least three cleanup macros are certified apply-capable.

todos.goal: Add local and targeted global control/data-flow analysis with sources, sinks, barriers, flow states, summaries, partial-flow diagnostics, and configurable library models.
acceptance: Local flow is the default fast path; global flow is source/sink bounded and budgeted.
Flow states, sanitizers, barriers, unknown calls, and external-library summaries are explicit.
Queries return path evidence and partial-flow explanations when no full path is proven.
Precision, recall, runtime, and memory are measured per language and query class.

todos.goal: Continuously ingest normalized traces, profiles, coverage, logs, feature-flag states, and probe observations into the evidence lattice without retaining sensitive payloads.
acceptance: Ingestion supports OpenTelemetry JSON/OTLP exports and a stable offline fixture format.
Payload values, secrets, personal data, and high-cardinality identifiers are redacted or hashed by policy.
Observations carry environment, build, commit, sampling, freshness, and coverage provenance.
Runtime evidence can strengthen or contradict static edges but cannot erase static uncertainty.

todos.goal: Synthesize minimal read-only probes that target unresolved graph edges and interface assumptions, execute them in allowlisted environments, and record evidence sufficient to discharge blockers.
acceptance: Probe synthesis starts from a named unresolved evidence obligation and stops when that obligation is discharged or budget expires.
Probes are read-only by contract, resource-bounded, network-allowlisted, and independently auditable.
Generated commands cannot interpolate untrusted repository content into shells.
Probe outcomes update only the evidence nodes they directly support.

todos.goal: Let projects define deterministic resolvers for dependency injection, reflection, plugin discovery, generated clients, routes, events, ORM models, decorators, metaprogramming, and build-time code generation.
acceptance: Resolvers declare applicable frameworks, versions, evidence inputs, output edge types, and trust requirements.
Resolver output is deterministic, scoped, provenance-rich, and independently replayable.
Conflicting resolvers produce an evidence conflict with no automatic winner.
Reference resolvers cover at least twelve widely used framework patterns.

todos.goal: Mine candidate runtime contracts from traces and compare before/after executions to verify routes, events, queries, latency, errors, resources, and side effects during macro simulation.
acceptance: Mined contracts remain provisional until reviewed or repeatedly observed across declared environments.
Differential verification separates semantic divergence from timing and infrastructure noise.
Canary sampling and replay fixtures are deterministic after normalization.
Runtime divergence automatically blocks macro promotion and records a minimized counterexample.

todos.goal: Build an incremental fleet graph spanning repositories, packages, services, owners, contracts, deployments, generated clients, release versions, and change campaigns.
acceptance: Repository identities and contract edges remain stable across clones, forks, and branch workspaces.
Access policy prevents facts from leaking between unauthorized repository partitions.
Cross-repository interface and deployment paths are queryable through MQL.
Index updates are content-addressed and avoid rescanning unchanged repositories.

todos.goal: Propagate API, event, schema, package, and configuration changes through producer and consumer repositories as ordered compatibility-preserving macro plans.
acceptance: Plans identify all known producers, consumers, generated clients, deployment constraints, and owner approvals.
Compatibility shims are installed before consumers migrate and removed only after completion evidence.
Partial repository access produces explicit unknown-consumer risk.
Rollback order respects published versions and deployed dependencies.

todos.goal: Execute certified macro campaigns across repository fleets with cohorting, concurrency, budgets, coordinated PRs, pause/resume, canaries, rollback, and campaign-level proof bundles.
acceptance: Campaigns start with read-only impact analysis and a deterministic cohort plan.
Canary failure pauses later cohorts and emits exact rollback or remediation choices.
Repository failures remain isolated while campaign state stays resumable and auditable.
SCM writes require explicit user intent and idempotency keys.

todos.goal: Turn takeover-init from a report into a guided deterministic workflow that creates a multi-file struct model, parser/resolver configuration, interface commitments, owners, test mappings, and an executable first optimization queue.
acceptance: The wizard is resumable, idempotent, non-destructive by default, and emits every proposed write as a macro plan.
It asks AI only for bounded domain intent, owner, or compatibility choices that cannot be derived.
Interface blocked states are discharged through concrete parser, runtime, owner, contract, or test evidence.
External benchmark repositories reach less than ten percent blocked interfaces after guided initialization.

todos.goal: Provide a local watch daemon that incrementally updates graph partitions, drift, interface commitments, affected tests, macro opportunities, and dashboard state as files and branches change.
acceptance: The daemon is optional, local-only by default, crash-safe, and never applies changes.
File events coalesce deterministically and invalidate only affected graph/test/report partitions.
Branch changes and rebases reset stale evidence without losing reusable content-addressed facts.
CLI and plugin commands transparently consume fresh daemon state or fall back to batch mode.

todos.goal: Deliver a production PR bot that explains structural impact, interface commitments, dynamic risks, selected tests, macro opportunities, proof status, waivers, and safe autofix plans without noisy duplicate comments.
acceptance: Bot comments are marker-updated, concise, stable, and link to machine-readable evidence artifacts.
Autofix PRs are created only from certified macros with explicit repository policy.
Fork, permission, secret, cancellation, rerun, stale-base, and concurrent-update cases are covered.
The bot reports why a test or reviewer was selected through graph paths.

todos.goal: Make project intelligence directly usable through terminal, Codex plugin, MCP, editor integrations, and stable deep links for graph queries, macro simulation, proof review, and approval queues.
acceptance: Users can ask structural questions and receive deterministic graph paths, evidence, and reproducible commands.
Interactive simulation never bypasses policy, certification, dirty-worktree, or write-intent gates.
Sessions can export compact handoff bundles that another agent or human can replay.
Terminal output remains the complete baseline; graphical clients are optional projections.

todos.goal: Create typed approval queues, owner delegation, separation of duties, expiring waivers, emergency stops, policy bundles, and auditable exception workflows for macro execution.
acceptance: Approvals bind exact content hashes, scopes, environments, expiry, identities, and proof bundles.
Changed plans invalidate prior approval automatically.
Emergency stop and revocation prevent pending and resumed apply operations.
Waivers cannot suppress false-safe-apply, rollback, provenance, or audit-log requirements.

todos.goal: Build a reproducible labeled corpus of at least 60 real repositories spanning languages, frameworks, monorepos, generated code, dynamic behavior, build systems, and project maturity levels.
acceptance: Corpus entries pin repository, commit, license, acquisition method, expected capabilities, and permitted redistribution.
Training/development and held-out evaluation partitions are separated and hash-locked.
The corpus includes at least six languages, twelve frameworks, ten monorepos, and fifteen dynamic-code cases.
Private repositories can use the same manifest contract without leaking source or identifiers.

todos.goal: Expand macro safety evaluation to at least 500 positive, negative, adversarial, composition, rollback, dynamic, concurrency, and cross-repository cases.
acceptance: Cases cover false matches, missed matches, stale captures, parser errors, dynamic uncertainty, effect conflicts, interrupted runs, and rollback corruption.
Held-out cases are not visible to macro synthesis or refinement.
Results report precision, recall, false-safe-apply, rollback, replay, runtime, and per-family confidence intervals.
Any false-safe-apply fails the release gate.

todos.goal: Evaluate months-long software evolution through ordered historical tasks, contract preservation, repeated upgrades, branch changes, failures, resumptions, and maintenance cost rather than isolated patches.
acceptance: Tasks are derived from real release histories and preserve chronological information boundaries.
Scoring includes completed intent, regressions, architecture drift, interface stability, human approvals, runtime, and rework.
Benchmark state can resume after interruption and verifies every checkpoint hash.
Macro-dominant runs are compared with manual-agent baselines under equal tool and time budgets.

todos.goal: Make daily intelligence cheap through partition caches, dependency-aware invalidation, worker isolation, deterministic concurrency, remote-cache adapters, and measured fast/full test economics.
acceptance: Cold, warm, one-file, one-symbol, branch-switch, and cache-corruption scenarios are measured separately.
Test selection reports saved time, cache trust, missed-test audits, and escalation reasons.
Parallel tasks use isolated output partitions and deterministic merge order.
Release tests verify both speed budgets and correctness under cache-disabled execution.
Contract, integration, benchmark, and release suites are labeled and routed independently; roadmap-only changes never trigger unrelated end-to-end autopilot runs.
Immutable benchmark and analysis artifacts are generated once per input digest and reused by downstream reports and plugin wrappers.
The test harness detects duplicate heavy phases in one run and reports per-check wall, user, and system time.

todos.goal: Sign macro packs, certificates, adapters, registry snapshots, and release artifacts with provenance and policy so external packs cannot silently alter execution behavior.
acceptance: Manifests bind source, build inputs, certificates, adapters, dependencies, permissions, and content hashes.
Unsigned or tampered packs can be inspected but cannot simulate or apply under trusted policy.
Key rotation, revocation, offline verification, and reproducible packaging are covered.
Release assets include machine-verifiable provenance.

todos.goal: Expose the same project graph, MQL, macro simulation, proof, and approval contracts through CLI, MCP, Codex plugin, CI, and adapters for other agent and refactoring ecosystems.
acceptance: All clients consume stable JSON contracts and receive equivalent deterministic decisions.
External tools contribute facts or edits only through declared adapter capabilities and provenance.
Model-specific prompts remain thin projections over the same control plane.
Conformance tests run without requiring proprietary cloud services.

todos.goal: Create the final v2 release gate that proves executable macro value, interface unblocking, dynamic evidence, external generality, long-horizon evolution, performance, hermetic replay, supply-chain integrity, and plugin usability.
acceptance: The gate verifies all program_targets from clean, cache-disabled, hermetic runs where applicable.
At least 24 macros are apply-capable, 40 simulate-capable, and 16 families are represented with zero false-safe-apply.
External and held-out evidence is required; self-optimization alone cannot satisfy readiness.
The plugin package includes signed maturity, benchmark, proof, performance, and compatibility summaries.
